title: The World's Oldest Pots
bullets: The Jomon people made fired clay pottery around 14,000 BCE, thousands of years before ceramics appeared in the Near East, making it among the oldest in the world.
The Jomon period lasted roughly 13,000 years, from 14,000 to 300 BCE, one of the longest continuous cultural traditions in human history, outlasting ancient Egypt about four times over.
Edward Sylvester Morse excavated the Omori Shell Mounds near Tokyo in 1877, the first scientific excavation in Japanese history, and coined the term Jomon meaning cord-marked.
Sannai-Maruyama in northern Honshu housed up to 500 people from roughly 3900 to 2300 BCE, with massive six-post chestnut-wood structures and long-distance trade in obsidian and jade.
Flame-style pottery from Niigata around 3500 BCE features rims exploding upward in writhing flame-like projections, the most visually dramatic ceramics in all of prehistory.
Over 18,000 dogu clay figurines have been found across Japan, with exaggerated goggle-like eyes and swollen limbs, probably fertility symbols or healing effigies that were deliberately broken.
Jade from Itoigawa on the Sea of Japan coast traveled over 300 miles to Sannai-Maruyama, possibly the earliest jade-working tradition in East Asia, predating Neolithic China.
Climatic cooling from 2000 to 1500 BCE reduced the nut-bearing forests and salmon runs that sustained Jomon prosperity, causing population contraction and setting the stage for the Yayoi arrival.

Modern Japanese people carry significant Jomon ancestry mixed with later continental arrivals, and some scholars argue Shinto preserves Jomon-era spiritual practices that survived every subsequent revolution.

title: The Yayoi Revolution
bullets: Wet-rice agriculture arrived from the Korean Peninsula around 900 BCE and concentrated sharply after 300 BCE, the most transformative event in Japanese history before the Meiji Restoration.
The earliest Yayoi rice paddies at Itazuke and Nabatake in northern Kyushu used techniques identical to contemporary rice farming on the Korean Peninsula.
Yayoi settlers were physically different from the Jomon, taller with narrower faces, and genetic studies confirm substantial migration from Korea mixed with the existing Jomon population.

Yoshinogari in northern Kyushu was a moated and palisaded town with watchtowers and elite jar-coffin burials, and Yayoi-period skeletons frequently show arrowheads embedded in bone and sword cuts on skulls.
Dotaku bronze bells, some over three feet tall, were elaborately decorated but never rung and had no clappers, buried on hilltops as ritual objects connected to the agricultural cycle.
In 57 CE a delegation from the Kingdom of Na received a gold seal from Chinese Emperor Guangwu inscribed King of Na of Wa, and the actual seal was dug up by a farmer in 1784.
Queen Himiko ruled the Yamatai confederation and sent envoys to Wei China in 238 CE, the first historically documented ruler of Japan, described as a shaman who bewitched the people.
Whether Yamatai was in northern Kyushu or the Kinai region near modern Nara has been debated for over a century, and the question may be unresolvable without new evidence.

title: The Tomb Builders
bullets: The Daisen Kofun near Osaka is roughly 1,600 feet long and covers about 80 acres, larger than the Great Pyramid of Giza by area, requiring an estimated 2,000 workers for over fifteen years.

Keyhole-shaped kofun burial mounds are a uniquely Japanese invention with no parallel in Korea, China, or anywhere else, and their form was only fully appreciated with twentieth-century aerial photography.
The Yamato court established dominance over much of Honshu and northern Kyushu through marriage alliances, ritual authority centered on the sun goddess Amaterasu, and military force.

Haniwa terracotta figures placed around kofun perimeters show warriors in riveted iron plate armor, armored horses, and elaborate court dress, providing invaluable visual evidence of Kofun-period life.
Korean toraijin immigrants brought iron smelting, silk weaving, sueki stoneware pottery, and horse breeding, and some scholars estimate one-third of aristocratic clans claimed Korean ancestry.
The scholar Wani reportedly arrived from Baekje around 405 CE carrying the Confucian Analects and the Thousand Character Classic, introducing Chinese characters to the Yamato court.

Chinese writing was spectacularly ill-suited to Japanese, a polysyllabic non-tonal inflected language completely different from Chinese, and Japan would spend four centuries wrestling with the problem.
Japan's island geography meant it could borrow from China through Korea without facing Chinese soldiers, a luxury that Korea, sitting on China's doorstep, rarely enjoyed.

title: The Borrowed Civilization
bullets: King Seong of Baekje sent a golden Buddha statue and sutras to the Yamato court around 552 CE, and the resulting clash between the pro-Buddhist Soga and pro-Shinto Mononobe clans ended with the Soga destroying the Mononobe in battle in 587.

Prince Shotoku served as regent from 593 to 622, promulgated the Seventeen-Article Constitution in 604, and sent an embassy to Sui China in 607 with a letter addressing the Chinese emperor as an equal.

Horyu-ji temple near Nara, founded by Prince Shotoku in 607, contains the oldest surviving wooden structures in the world, with a pagoda whose hanging central pillar acts as a passive tuned mass damper.
In 645 Prince Naka no Oe and Nakatomi no Kamatari assassinated Soga no Iruka at a court ceremony, ending Soga dominance and launching the Taika Reform modeled on Tang Chinese government.
Nakatomi no Kamatari received the surname Fujiwara as his reward for the 645 coup, founding a clan that would dominate Japanese politics for the next four centuries.

At the Battle of Baekgang in 663, a combined Yamato-Baekje fleet of over 400 ships was destroyed by Tang-Silla forces, ending Japanese military involvement on the Korean Peninsula for nearly a thousand years.
The Taiho Code of 701 codified provinces, districts, centralized taxation, and a bureaucratic rank system, giving Japan on paper a government as sophisticated as any in East Asia.
By 700 CE the Japanese court had borrowed its religion from India, writing from China, architecture from Korea, and legal code from China, yet the result was unmistakably Japanese.

title: Nara
bullets: Heijo-kyo was laid out as a copy of Tang China's Chang'an with a grid of straight streets and the palace at the northern end, but unlike Chang'an it had no walls because island geography eliminated the need for defense.
O no Yasumaro completed the Kojiki in 712, Japan's oldest surviving written work recording the mythological origins of Japan from the divine couple Izanagi and Izanami through Emperor Jimmu.
The Nihon Shoki, completed in 720, was Japan's first official national history written entirely in classical Chinese and included alternative versions of many myths that the Kojiki omitted.
Emperor Shomu ordered a national network of kokubunji provincial temples each with a seven-story pagoda, the religious equivalent of garrisoning the country with prayer.
The Great Buddha at Todai-ji in Nara stood roughly 49 feet tall, consumed most of Japan's copper and gold reserves, and was dedicated in 752 at a ceremony attended by over 10,000 monks.

The Shoso-in repository at Todai-ji has preserved over 9,000 objects for 1,200 years including Persian glass, Tang mirrors, Central Asian textiles, and Indian instruments, a time capsule of the Silk Road.
The Man'yoshu compiled around 759 contains over 4,500 poems by more than 500 authors from emperors to frontier soldiers, using Chinese characters purely as phonetic sound symbols.
The monk Dokyo nearly seized the throne in the 760s through his influence over the reigning empress, and Emperor Kanmu moved the capital to Kyoto in 794 to escape Nara's overpowered clergy.

title: The Heian Court
bullets: In 894 Sugawara no Michizane recommended canceling embassies to Tang China because the dynasty was visibly declining, beginning Japan's period of relative cultural independence.

After Michizane's death in exile in 903, plagues and lightning strikes convinced the court his angry spirit was taking revenge, so they deified him as Tenjin, the god of learning, still worshipped today.
The Fujiwara clan monopolized power by marrying their daughters to emperors; Fujiwara no Michinaga dominated court from 995 to 1027, seeing three grandsons become emperors in succession.
By around 850 Japanese scribes had simplified Chinese characters into hiragana and katakana, two phonetic syllabaries of about fifty symbols each that could write any Japanese word.

Kukai founded Shingon Buddhism at Mount Koya in 816 and is traditionally credited with creating kana; his contemporary Saicho founded Tendai at Mount Hiei, establishing the two great Buddhist institutions.
Murasaki Shikibu completed The Tale of Genji around 1010, over 1,000 pages covering 75 years with 400 characters, widely considered the world's first novel, seven centuries before Europe's.
Sei Shonagon completed The Pillow Book around 1002, a collection of lists, anecdotes, and observations that essentially invented the essay form a thousand years before the internet.

The Engishiki of 927 codified over 2,800 official Shinto shrines across Japan, and the coexistence of Shinto and Buddhism called shinbutsu-shugo lasted over a thousand years until the Meiji government forcibly separated them in the 1870s.

title: Warriors Rising
bullets: The Heian court abolished the Chinese-style conscript army in 792 and replaced it with nothing, delegating provincial defense to local strongmen who became the ancestors of the samurai.
The Taira and Minamoto clans, both claiming descent from junior imperial branches, provided the military muscle the court could not supply, becoming the two dominant warrior houses.
The Former Nine Years' War from 1051 to 1062 in northern Honshu cemented Minamoto military prestige but taught the court that pacifying the provinces required warriors with independent power.

The Fujiwara regency was partially displaced by insei, cloistered rule, where retired emperors exercised power from behind the scenes free from the ceremonial obligations constraining the reigning emperor.

In 1053 Fujiwara no Yorimichi built the Phoenix Hall at Byodo-in, a masterpiece of Heian architecture depicted on the 10-yen coin, representing Buddhist paradise as the Fujiwara political paradise was crumbling.
The Hogen Rebellion of 1156 saw samurai fight in the streets of Kyoto for the first time, shattering the pretense that the court could maintain order without warrior involvement.

The rise of provincial warriors in eleventh-century Japan parallels the feudal transformation in Western Europe, where mounted knights were similarly building local power through landholding and military service.

title: The Genpei War
bullets: After the Heiji Rebellion of 1160 the Taira spared two young Minamoto sons, a decision they would regret more than any other in Japanese history: Yoritomo and Yoshitsune grew up to destroy them.
Taira no Kiyomori became the first warrior to hold the rank of Grand Minister in 1167 and married his daughter to the emperor, controlling the court through Fujiwara-style marriage politics.
Minamoto no Yoritomo spent twenty years in exile at Izu building alliances, then raised the banner of revolt in 1180 when Prince Mochihito called for arms against the Taira.
Yoshitsune led a mounted charge down a near-vertical cliff at Ichi-no-Tani in 1184, attacked the Taira naval base from land at Yashima, and commanded the decisive fleet at Dan-no-ura in 1185.
At Dan-no-ura the Taira matriarch Tokiko took the eight-year-old Emperor Antoku in her arms and leapt into the sea, and the sacred sword of the imperial regalia was lost forever.
The Tale of the Heike opens with a passage every educated Japanese person knows: the sound of the Gion Shoja bells echoes the impermanence of all things, the foundational epic of samurai culture.
Yoritomo turned on his own brother Yoshitsune, accusing him of insubordination, hunting him across Japan until Yoshitsune killed his family and committed suicide in 1189 at age thirty.
Yoshitsune became Japan's most tragic hero, the origin of hogan-biiki, sympathy for the doomed noble loser, a concept that defines a distinctive Japanese cultural sensibility.
